title: Chapter 6: Flame and Brotherhood
paragraphs: They set up camp in a deep, sheltered crevice, under the boughs of a petrified giant tree whose branches resembled the intertwined fingers of a corpse. Avotu, without even turning his head, ignited the collected brushwood with a flick of his finger. Black flame licked the dry wood, turning into an ordinary, life-saving red-orange fire. The strangers sat around, huddling close to each other. Their faces were covered in dirt and soot.

Ravan sat a little apart, clutching his knee with his healthy hand. His broken right hand was a jagged, purple mess. He didn't make a sound, but cold sweat rolled down his tensed neck. The pain was monstrous, but even more unbearable was the feeling of his own weakness. He, a huge and strong man, had proved to be a toy in the teeth of a local monster.
Avotu slowly approached him. There was no condescension in the elf's eyes. He silently dropped to one knee and intercepted the man's injured forearm with an iron grip.

"Endure, mortal," Avotu whispered. Black, oily flame slid from the elf's hands, enveloping the broken hand. A sickening wet crunch echoed: under the influence of primal magic, the bone fragments with force, driving themselves back, stood in place. It was not a merciful magic of healing; it was a cruel reconstruction. Ravan growled in pain, biting his lip to blood, but Alexander was immediately there, firmly gripping his healthy shoulder.

"I'm with you, brother. Hold on," Sasha whispered. Zhenya knelt nearby, and Yura and Anar froze in suspense, ready at any moment to support Ravan. This cohesion, this animal, brotherly love hit Avotu in the gut again. When the fire died out, Ravan's wrist was anatomically whole, only covered with a thin layer of whitish scar tissue.
Alexander wiped his forehead with the back of his hand and looked at the elf.
"Thank you. For everything," he said sincerely. "You don't have to deal with us. You're on the edge yourself, I can see it. You're as broken as we are. Why don't you leave us?"

Avotu stared into the dancing tongues of a flame for a long time. Deep inside, he struggled with the desire to close up, as he had been doing for the last two years. But these people... they broke through his armor with their simplicity and devotion to each other.

"Loneliness kills more surely than a demon's fangs," the fire elf said hoarsely, with a break. The shadows on his face thickened, giving away a phantom pain of loss. "I am looking for my family. My brothers-in-arms. I believed that at least one of them was alive. Но сегодня, когда я смотрел на вас... I realized that something valuable still remains in this rotting world. You are ready to die for each other."
A heavy silence fell. The strangers looked at each other. No one began to shower loud words; one look was enough to understand—they would not fail this tormented hero.
"So, now we are your family too," Evgeniy summarized surlily, but firmly.

Avotu turned away sharply, hiding the emotions that flared in his eyes. He shifted his gaze to Anar, who had already settled down comfortably on a pile of dry leaves, pulling his collar up to his nose.
"Get up," the elf's voice had again acquired steel, teacherly notes.
"Listen, I've already thrown lightning today," Anar muttered, reluctantly opening one eye. "My heroism limit for this week is exhausted. I'm lazy."

"Tomorrow there may be two creatures. Или три. If you burn your nerve nodes, not knowing how to control the release of mana, you will die. А если ты умрешь, who will protect them?" Avotu nodded at Ravan and Yura. It was a blow below the belt.

Anar sighed heavily, swore quietly and rose reluctantly, shaking off his pants. Avotu showed the basic elven stance and explained the principle of driving mana through the joints for acceleration. What happened next shocked everyone. Anar, whose laziness was legendary, awkwardly shifted his weight... and the air around him exploded with the smell of a storm.

His silhouette blurred into a blue trace. With a dry crack, he was behind Avotu's back, jokingly putting a finger to his neck. The 'sloth's' speed was absolutely inhuman, instinctive. He was a natural genius who didn't need cramming.
"Okay, I figured out the mechanics. I'm going to sleep," Anar immediately yawned and fell back onto the leaves, leaving Avotu in mixed feelings of admiration and irritation.

On the other side of the camp, Zhenya, far from magic, found peace in metal. Using a sharpened stone, the soot-smeared worker from his past life methodically disassembled a demonic mechanism taken from one of the corpses. A red crystal shimmered dimly inside. Magic was frightening him, but levers, gears and the laws of pneumatics were his element. Connecting a copper bracket with a valve, Zhenya made the mechanism hum weakly.
Avotu, watching them, felt the black flame on his head become a little warmer. For the first time in two years, he didn't want to disappear. He wanted these people to live.
